comments.author: @World_Untold_321
comments.text: This isn't just a travel documentary; it's a poetic masterclass in history, economics, and human resilience. The storytelling, the 4K visuals, and the pacing are absolutely flawless.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Thank you — that means a lot. Portugal's turnaround gave us a lot to work with. What surprised you most about how it happened?

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Thank you for trusting us with a piece of your story - a life that began with so little and built so much deserves to be honored, and yours clearly has. Wishing you continued health, and thank you for watching.

comments.author: @sergiolima444
comments.text: Portugal future is bright. This society was castrated of its individual potential by a dictatorship that lasted decades that infected and indoctrinated the minds of the people with fear and low self esteem. The regime is gone, but as often happens, the country is rebuilding itself away from that mindset. It will still take decades and generations to completly dilutte it into something else. Its an ongoing process, but we are doing it, and Portugal will be great again

replies.author: @appstratum9747
replies.text:
Absolutely. This is what so many people coming from countries that have never experienced the national trauma of such a long lasting dictatorship often find difficult to understand.  With every passing year, though, I see Portugal healing that wound. The country and the Portuguese people have changed markedly since I first arrived from the UK to work here 30 years ago.

When I first came here - particularly in the rural areas - you could see three different generations of Portuguese. The old people were for the most part pretty timid and beaten down. In regions like Alentejo where the regime had restricted education to just four years to cripple any meaningful political opposition, that generation were powerless to change anything and they knew it. It had become so internalised that most people of that generation - even in communist areas - largely believed Salazar when in so many words he effectivelysaid that the Portuguese were a stupid people that needed somebody like him to lead them.

The generation that had been teenagers and in their early twenties at the time of the revolution were largely responsible for turning Portugal from a poverty-stricken and illiterate dictatorship into an imperfect democracy that was modernising quickly by 1997/98 and Expo 98. Yet many of this generation progressed largely because of who they knew rather than on merit. And many were deeply insecure about their jobs and lack of qualifications.

Children born in the eighties and nineties had no recollection at all of the dictatorship and couldn't really imagine what that was like. Many, I felt, were quite spoiled by parents who worked all hours and spent little time with their children. That was certainly true of many of the Portuguese that I worked with. And it was certainly true of my own (Portuguese) stepson. But this generation and the generation that has come after it have the expectation, at least, that Portugual should be a meritocracy. They're far better educated and far more widely traveled and knowledgeable. And justifiable in their frustration when it comes to poor wages/conditions when housing is so expensive and they know conditions are often better abroad. But for the first time in so many years, whilst many accept that they're likely to be financially and materially better off abroad, most - if conditions were better - would actually like to stay in Portugal. Because Portugal is a very nice country in which to live....if you can afford it. Foreigners can. Many young Portuguese in coastal cities cannot. Hence the frustration.

I share your optimism about a bright future for Portugal. To me, as a Brit (who is ever more Portuguese, as you'd expect after so many years here) I regard the indignant frustration of young Portuguese who are struggling to buy a home in their own areas a very positive sign. Gone is the meek acceptance of hardship by their grandparents under Salazar. They want better. They know that they deserve better. And they're damn sure that they want better for their children. And are educated and capable enough to make this happen on their own merits.

Yes it's an ongoing process. But Portugal continues to go in the right direction. And each new generation that I've seen - all now feeling just as European as any German, Dane, Belgian or Swede - are making the necessary changes. Many without really knowing that this is what they're doing. Gone is the low self esteem in in these young people. That's still there in some older people. But the young Portuguese are a different breed entirely. And that's such a good thing I have just needed to wipe a tear from my eye.

That tear welled up in my eye because I remember my very humble and downtrodden but exceptionally kind elderly neighbours in a small village in rural Portugal. These people had very little self-esteem, survived on hardly any money but got exceptional pleasure in offering eggs from their chickens to the younger British/Portuguese couple living next door. Neither of them could read nor write very well. They were functionally illiterate. Their grandson became a senior official in the UK's banking regulator in London and later a senior manager at the headquarters of Santander bank in Madrid.

This is the wide gap that Portugal has stepped across in the last two generations. I got emotional just thinking about that just now. But it's one of the reasons that I'm proud to live here and to be part of this story.

The future of Portugal is bright. We just need to keep working towards that. And to do it together.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: That's a thoughtful way to put it — untangling decades of one mindset doesn't happen on a single generation's timeline. Thank you for adding that context.
